macro_segments.title: Introduction to Hataras Nedarim: Charata vs. Pesach as grounds for release

micro_segments.title: Two foundational concepts: charata (regret) and pesach (retroactive disavowal based on error)
The question of whether one must open with pesach or whether simple charata suffices

macro_segments.title: Case of Rabbi Yannai: Using the heavenly ledger inspection as a pesach

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yannai's grandson comes seeking release; Rabbi Yannai opens with the idea that vows trigger divine scrutiny
Rabbi Abba cites the verse 'acharei nedarim levaker' (after vows you will be inspected) as scriptural basis
Analysis: This pesach is too generic; it works for any vow-taker without case-specific reasoning

macro_segments.title: Rejection of scare-tactic pesakim: The heavenly ledger and the altar/sacrifice metaphors

micro_segments.title: The Gemara rules: we do not open even with Rabbi Yannai's heavenly ledger inspection because it is too generic and a scare tactic
We also do not open with Rabbi Natan's statement that one who vows is like building an altar outside the Temple
Extended analysis of the bamah metaphor: vows as unauthorized personal religious practice vs. communal Torah observance
The Ran explains: the vow-taker mistakenly thinks self-imposed prohibitions are a mitzvah, but adding to Torah prohibitions is sinful
Debate between Abaye and Rava: whether we open with the bamah statement at all; final halakha is we do not open with either beginning or end

macro_segments.title: Prohibition against using scare-tactic pesakim; additional rejected statements about vow-takers as wicked

micro_segments.title: We do not open with Shmuel's statement that even one who fulfills a vow is called wicked
Rabbi Abbahu cites the verse 'vchi techdal lindor lo yihyeh becha cheit' to show that ceasing vows removes sin
Rav Yosef notes: even the sages taught this idea, using parallels to the vows of wicked vs. righteous people

macro_segments.title: Digression on anger and its consequences in Talmudic thought

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yonatan/Yochanan: anyone who gets angry has all forms of Gehinnom controlling him
Verse 'haseir kaas milibecha veha'aveir ra'ah mibsarecha' interpreted: removing anger removes evil from flesh; evil = Gehinnom
Tangential effects of anger: hemorrhoids (chattoniyot) as physical manifestation of internal burning
Story of two men from Chuza accompanying Ulla to Eretz Yisrael: one murders the other in anger
Ulla tells the murderer 'yes' out of fear; later admits to Rabbi Yochanan this reinforced a sinner's hands
Rabbi Yochanan challenges: the verse promises an angry heart only in exile (Babylon); how could this happen before crossing the Jordan?
Multiple statements on anger's consequences: forgetting Torah, increasing foolishness, indicating abundant sin
Rav Ada's statement: had Israel not sinned at the spies (cheit hamiraglim), only Torah and Joshua would have been needed

macro_segments.title: Vows made in the name of the God of Israel: Distinction between neder and shvua; exception for shalom bayis

micro_segments.title: Rav Assi: we do not attend to vows made in the name of 'Elohei Yisrael' except in cases of shalom bayis
Case of woman who vowed her husband cannot benefit from her; she stole his wallet and hit his son
Interpretive question: does 'Elohei Yisrael' refer to a neder or shvua? Ran vs. Rashi perspectives
Rav Assi to woman: had you vowed with 'mohi' (euphemism/kinuy for oath), I would help you; but with 'Elohei Yisrael,' I cannot
Story of Rav Kahana and Rav Yosef: guest refuses food in name of 'God,' host refuses to feed in same name—showing casual use of oaths

macro_segments.title: Bottom-line halakha and Geonic dispute: Can we open with charata? Can we release shvuot?

micro_segments.title: Rav Nachman's ruling: we open with charata (simple regret) and we attend to vows made in the name of God
Ran's clarification: we open with charata for both neder and shvua; we release even shvuot in the name of God
Geonic pushback: unnamed Gaon forbids release of vows taken in God's name; this is rejected as against the Gemara
Rav Hai Gaon's stricter position: open only with pesach (not charata) for nedarim; never release shvuot except for shalom bayis
Mar Yehudai Gaon even stricter: treat nedarim like shvuot; never release either unless there is a davar mitzvah or shalom bayis
Conclusion: Rambam and Shulchan Aruch pasken according to the Gemara, not the Geonim; we always release both nedarim and shvuot

topical_tags.term: Shalom bayis (peace between husband and wife)
Piu v'libo shavin (mouth and heart in agreement)
